The Preacher forces us to witness an obscenity that no amount of success can hide: a man drowning in abundance. God grants him everything his appetite demands—wealth, honor, children, length of days—yet withholds the one thing that matters: the ability to enjoy it. Someone else will inherit what he labored to build. His accomplishments turn to ash in his own hands. This is not mere disappointment. This is affliction. The Preacher compares such a man to a stillborn child and concludes the stillborn child is better off. A child who never drew breath at least never knew the torment of grasping at satisfaction it could never hold. The child never saw the sun; the man with a hundred children and a thousand years of life might as well be that child, for neither one finds rest. Both end in darkness. Both reach "the same place." The brutality here is intentional. The Preacher is not speaking in hyperbole to make a point about gratitude. He is naming something many of us refuse to admit: that having everything according to the world's measure can be worse than having nothing, because it convinces us we should be happy while leaving us hollowed out.

What distinguishes the wise man from the fool, then? What does the poor man gain by learning to conduct himself well in society? In a world where satisfaction cannot be found—where labor fills the mouth but never satisfies the appetite, where every achievement belongs to someone else in the end—wisdom offers no escape hatch. The eye can see what is present before it, which is "better" than the wandering of desire that chases phantoms. But this modest superiority is itself "futile and a pursuit of the wind." The Preacher will not let us take refuge even in the virtue of contentment or restraint. There is no hiding place in human wisdom, because the terms themselves are fixed beyond our control. Everything that exists was named long ago. What man is was determined before any of us drew breath. We cannot contend with the One stronger than ourselves—the One who set these terms and watches them unfold.

The conclusion is almost unbearable in its honesty: for who knows what is genuinely good during the few days we shadow through existence? And who can tell what comes after? We are not equipped to answer these questions. We have no vantage point from which to see our own lives whole. We live in ignorance both of what serves us and of what awaits us. Yet notice what the Preacher has just established: God does exist. God gives and withholds. God determines outcomes. God is stronger than we are. The one thing the Preacher refuses to say is that none of this matters, or that God is absent. Instead, he has methodically stripped away every false comfort—success, descendants, years, wisdom, restraint—and left us standing before the face of reality itself. This is the only ground on which genuine faith can be built: not the hope that effort will be rewarded, but the stark recognition that we are held by Someone we did not create, in a world we do not control, during days we do not own. What you cannot fix or understand or secure for yourself—perhaps that is precisely where you will meet Him.
